author: śrīla saccidānanda bhaktibinoda ṭhākura
verses:
varaja⋅vipine jamunā⋅kūle
mañca manohara śobhita phule

vanaspati latā tuṣaye ā̐khi
tadupari kata ḍākaye pākhī

malaya anila vahaye dhīre
alikula madhu⋅ lobhete phire

vāsantīra rākā⋅ uḍupa tadā
kaumudī vitare ādare sadā

emata samaye rasikavara
ārambhila rāsa muralīdhara

śatakoṭī gopī⋅ mājhete hari
rādhā⋅saha nāce ānanda kari’

mādhava⋅mohinī gāiyā gīta
harila sakala jagata cita

sthāvara⋅jaṅgama mohilā satī
hārāola candrā⋅ valīra matī

mathiyā varaja⋅ kiśora mana
antarita haya rādhā takhana

bhakativinoda paramāda gaṇe
rāsa bhāṅgala (āji) rādhā vihane
en_translation:
In the groves of Vraja, on the banks of the Yamunā, there is an enchanting platform beautifully decorated with flowers.

The sight of the majestic trees and creepers delights the eyes, while many birds chirp upon their branches.

The gentle Malayan breeze blows softly, and swarms of bees hover around, greedy for honey.

The full moon of spring distributes its moonlight with constant affection.

At such a time, the best of connoisseurs, the Holder of the Flute, began the rāsa dance.

In the midst of hundreds of millions of gopīs, Hari dances with Rādhā in great joy.

Singing Her songs, the Enchantress of Mādhava stole the hearts of everyone in the world.

That chaste lady enchanted all moving and non-moving beings, and even Candrāvalī lost her composure.

Having churned the mind of the Youth of Vraja, Rādhā then suddenly disappeared.

Bhaktivinoda considers this a great calamity; today the rāsa dance has ended in the absence of Rādhā.
